findings.detail: 4 nodes (within 3–8). Bold labels all ≤ 4 words: 'Low variation' (2 words) and 'High variation' (2 words) in both subgraphs. All four labels + subtitle interpretations trace directly to corpus §5.3 quadrant descriptions: Q1='Confident model — most trustworthy' (corpus: 'Strongest signal of model confidence. Most trustworthy for factual tasks.'); Q2='Reliability warning — model uncertain' (corpus: 'Reliability warning — model's internal probability mass is genuinely spread.'); Q3='Surprisingly robust — strong preference' (corpus: 'Surprisingly strong confidence signal — preference so strong it overrides the spread distribution.'); Q4='Expected — variety is fine here' (corpus: 'Does not signal unreliability — signals the task has many valid answers.'). No bare unexplained acronyms. Governing concept lives in the title: config field.

flowchart LR with two side-by-side subgraphs (LOW_TEMP left, HIGH_TEMP right), each using direction TB to stack their two variation nodes vertically. This produces a genuine 2×2 grid: columns = temperature axis (low/high), rows = variation axis (low/high). Subgraph titles serve as column headers, matching the 2×2 grid intent. The ~~~ invisible link keeps the two columns side-by-side without unwanted edges. ClassDef palette: done=green (Q1, most trustworthy), hitl=yellow/amber (Q2, reliability warning — warns the human), act=blue (Q3, robust/trustworthy but unexpected), auto=purple (Q4, expected automated variation).

Palette reused for semantic meaning: green (done) = reliable/go signal; amber (hitl) = warning/human should notice; blue (act) = action-relevant positive signal; purple (auto) = expected automated outcome. No pipeline-role misuse — these are repurposed for reliability signal semantics in this non-pipeline diagram, which is acceptable since there are no actual HITL gates or auto steps to misrepresent.
